title: A new perspective on Shinji and Asukas relation in NTE
display_title: A new perspective on Shinji and Asukas relation in NTE
wikitext:
[]: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Wed Jun 12, 2024 10:40 pm I will start this by addressing the obvious: Shinji and Asuka's relationship is not as well developed and relevant as the original series'. This is not me trying to compare the two. It isn't about shipping either. I just want to point out some elements people usually overlook and maybe suggest an interpretation. I want to start by pointing out how their relationship subverts the one from the original series. In NGE, Asuka starts the series not really liking Shinji, but the feeling gradually increases as they spend time together. I guess you could say it's similar to how these feelings play out in real life. But the irony is that the more she likes him, the more they grow apart and the more she pushes him away. In the movies, however, she starts to like him very quickly and even tries to compete with Rei for his attention at some point. It's a lot more artificial at a first glance, but it makes sense when you consider the environment she grew up in (competitive place, no one really cared about her as a person, etc). That being said, as the movies go on, she starts to lose interest (the complete opposite of the OG story). Of course, there wasn't proper time to develop their friendship, but this more "automatized" relationship fits well with the themes of Asuka's character. She herself is an artificial creation, learning to be her (just like Rei VI in the last film). Because of it, their first contact is sillier in nature and only gains more nuance as she develops as a person and he grows. Even though, in HA, Asuka's feelings were presented in a way that made them look like a typical teen romance, I think it meant so much more to her. Shinji is the first in the movie to treat Asuka as a human first and a pilot second. In flashbacks we see that she was disposable in the eyes of others for most of her life. But Shinji treated her with kindness and friendship. I think it's why his bentōs meant so much to her. It was a gesture of care Shinji had for her. He conects with people via cooking (something Rei was also inspired by). In the original series I think Asuka liked Shinji because they were very similar. But in films it's the opposite. Asuka liked Shinji because he is different from her and the others. There is a role reversal to the point where Shinji is the one who makes the final confession, something most of the audience expected Asuka to do. From his point of view, these feelings are still much more tangible (14 years ago, not that long ago from his point of view). It's a genuine gesture, unlike EOE (when Shinji did the same because he was literally going crazy, for lack of a better term). As for her part, there is always a deep desire for connection and sharing of pain. At first she sees him as a romantic partner, then as someone who needs protection, a mother. Even after he was gone, she kept thinking about him. Not in the same way, but he marked her. When she finally talks to him and tells him how she felt in the past, it's a huge step for her character, something she wouldn't have done before. And when Shinji does the same, I believe she was surprised at the idea of ​​someone else reciprocating those same feelings for her in the past. To be more precise, I think her reaction comes from the shock of knowing, not that she necessarily still likes him that way. For me, she sees him as a good memory and perhaps a what if. That old friend we all have, I guess. There's more to it than that, especially when it comes to their origins, but I think this is the bigger picture. I don't see it as "bad" anymore.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by C.T.1290 » Thu Jun 13, 2024 12:56 am Well, I don’t really consider Shikinami as the real Asuka, seeing as she’s not the original one. So at that point, I think that Mari is more or less her replacement, one with a more healthier relationship. I still see Soryu as the only real Asuka in the entire series, or this case, the whole franchise. So probably at some point in the post Third Impact world, I think that Shinji and Asuka may have time to work out their issues and maybe fix themselves in the process before maybe making amends for hurting each other. That of course is up to interpretation.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Thu Jun 13, 2024 6:21 pm I don't really get why Shikinami would be a "fake Asuka". Soryu is the original character, but Shikinami is Asuka in the new movies. She is just as much Asuka as any other Asuka in any other media. Not to say she's as well written as her original counterpart, but she's an official character.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by C.T.1290 » Fri Jun 14, 2024 12:41 am BernardoCairo wrote: I don't really get why Shikinami would be a "fake Asuka". Soryu is the original character, but Shikinami is Asuka in the new movies. She is just as much Asuka as any other Asuka in any other media. Not to say she's as well written as her original counterpart, but she's an official character.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Kendrix » Thu Dec 12, 2024 9:10 pm I wish I could find that one article where Asuka's VA talks about how she would consider Rebuild Asuka a tsundere since she does like Shinji, while OG Asuka didn't/ the person she likes was Kaji. Rebuild Asuka basicaly likes Shinji for the same reason that OG Asuka likes Kaji: "The bento was good so I thought I liked you"

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Asunji_Yuko » Thu Jan 09, 2025 7:26 pm Kendrix wrote: I wish I could find that one article where Asuka's VA talks about how she would consider Rebuild Asuka a tsundere since she does like Shinji, while OG Asuka didn't/ the person she likes was Kaji.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Fri Jan 10, 2025 9:59 am Asunji_Yuko wrote: It's also obvious that OG Asuka liked Shinji when you look at the director's cut of episode 22.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by FelipeFritschF » Fri Jan 10, 2025 6:10 pm I think Asuka's character really did outgrow herself in the course of production of NGE. In the Proposal stage, Asuka did seem like Shinji's love interest, but there was no clear indication that Rei wouldn't be just as pronounced in that regard. One could be forgiven for Shinji would end up with Rei by the end of ep 6, and then Anno "forgets about Rei", and she barely does anything for a bunch of episodes, even though Rei's whole character arc in the series in just as fantastic and well developed. Tsurumaki mentioned Misato as being the actual deutaragonist in the EoE booklet interview and that was probably their initial intention. Then we have Anno taking a big liking towards Asuka over time, and I get the feeling this was so significant he ended up further developing her and givin her a larger role, especially for EoE. The DC episodes themselves are a good example of this. Before their additions, you could still make a reasonable argument that Asuka wasn't *that* into Shinji, but after the much expanded mental attack sequences? Yeah, she likes him alright. Then of course we have EoE itself. NTE is an extension of that, of course. (I can point out to all the specific interviews later if anyone is so interested. No time right now.)

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Weird_ocean » Sat Jan 11, 2025 3:10 am I think we simply don't have enough of information about Asuka's true feelings in Shin. She has very limited exposure, and those moments are so subtle that one can basically imagine anything. Unpopular opinion, but I think in The Minus-Sapce Asuka scene, Shinji was the one wearing Kensuke's doll, like a costume, because it was later shown at the studio with Rei scene (evangelions there were hanging off the wall, unzipped like literal costumes, as to make a tokusatsu movie, and with ships as models). I think that, because in the Minus-Space, whenever people speak to one another, it's not some visions or illusion, but actual people or their souls in the minus space. And because Kensuke could not be there, Shinji took his place, because he needed to remind Asuka about a place where she can belong. Asuka never sees Kensuke's shack in her memories, but she does see Shinji's room, and an aquarium, and that tells me that she might have been more attached to Shinji, than to Kensuke. He also says same words to Asuka in Japanese that he said to Rei Lookalike "Asuka wa Asuka da", and calls her Asuka, which Kensuke never did, he only called her Shikinami. But that's up for interpretation. In my opinion, Shinji did it, to break their bond, to get her out of the minus space, and save her, before he could commit seppuku with a spear. And that fits theme of the movie, because no one in the Rebuilds can save themselves, there is always someone else that does it. So if Asuka would just came to realization about Kensuke on her own, that would contradict the whole message. And Shinji had to do it. Now, to the point of Shinji and Asuka's relationships, They never were a thing in Rebuilds, and they never actually tried to connect in a meaningful way as a couple. Yes, they were romantically interested in one another (although we never really see that coming from Shinji in Rebuilds) and yes that is in the past, and they needed to close the page. But effectively they never actually dated or had proper relationships. And that is also the case in NGE. They never dated properly. And when Shinji comes back to reality, I don't see why they can't try and start with knowing each other better, and then who knows, being friends and\or lovers is a possibility. Especially now, when they left all their traumas behind them. Now they have more chance than ever, in my opinion. I'm saying this because of people that I know in real life. I have friends who had terrible problems in relationships and were on the brink of divorce, but worked on their issues, and stayed together and are happy now. I knew a couple who got divorced and was separated for more than a decade, but then got together again, and are now happy. When you have problems in relationships, breaking up, and ending it all is not the only option, and sometimes not even the best one. If Shinji and Asuka in the end of Shin, left their juvenile selves in the past, and are adults in both minds and bodies, they have more in common than ever before. I'm wondering how this new Asuka and Shinji will see each other when they meet again. They will be different people in many ways, and that opens up a lot of possibilities. including Kensuke's and Shinji's rivalry over Asuka. She also might not be interested in any of them romantically at all. All I want to say is, we can imagine all of those things without a problem, because Shin leaves us enough room to do that.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by dzzthink » Fri Jan 17, 2025 6:53 pm From my perspective, the rebuild tells a story about a relationship that did not work out despite strong feelings between each other. It's the typical unrequited love scenario, or Romeo and Juliet where it's a doomed romance due to circumstance. I recently found an article on how Anno was a bit of a simp for a VA ( https://www.cbr.com/evangelion-hideaki- ... irst-love/ ). It just goes to show a type of crush that is not realistic in terms of an actual relationship.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by JFFF-7292 » Thu Mar 27, 2025 11:24 am There are three important differences between NGE+EoE and Rebuild regarding the A/S relationship. 1 - Shikinami is not Soryu, they have a different past, they see Shinji differently. 2 - in Rebuild, in all three films (excluding 1.11), Shinji never shows a romantic interest towards Asuka (evidently for Hideaki Anno this was not supposed to be the main theme of Rebuild, there was not enough time). So Shikinami does not have the same role as Soryu. It is only at the end of 3.0+1.01 that it is revealed that Shinji was attracted to Asuka. 3 - the 14 year time jump that creates a partial gap between Asuka and Shinji and complicates the situation. About Asuka: Kendrix wrote: in the final fight in Q she's even ready to kill him ("just shut up and die stupid brat!") - she wonders what she ever saw in the guy. But in the end when he's curled into a ball and not resisting, she can't bring herself to kill him or leave him for dead. In part because she thinks dying is too good for him (as she tells Kensuke) but probably also because of some genuine lingering attachment (which Mari repeatedly teases her about)

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Rigel » Wed May 14, 2025 10:29 am BernardoCairo wrote: And when Shinji does the same, I believe she was surprised at the idea of someone else reciprocating those same feelings for her in the past. To be more precise, I think her reaction comes from the shock of knowing, not that she necessarily still likes him that way. For me, she sees him as a good memory and perhaps a what if. That old friend we all have, I guess.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Thu May 22, 2025 8:35 am This is actually a really good angle that I hadn't considered. My opinion of the scene has changed a lot over time. I've become more accepting of the idea that Asuka still had feelings for Shinji, since she's a fictional character. I think trying to impose real-life logic on her emotions doesn't really help the discussion. At the end of the day, she was trapped as a 14-year-old girl. I think her feelings for Shinji played a role in that. And I don't necessarily think the story paints that in a negative light.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by JFFF-7292 » Fri May 23, 2025 5:17 am BernardoCairo wrote: I think trying to impose real-life logic on her emotions doesn't really help the discussion. At the end of the day, she was trapped as a 14-year-old girl.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun May 25, 2025 12:10 am I think this topic is as good as any for dipping my toes back into forum posting: When it comes to examining Asuka as a character in Rebuild, as well as Shinji's relationship with her, we have to take stock in the dynamics that remained the same and were carried forward from NGE. An important part of this process is also reconsidering how Asuka's characterization in Rebuild retroactively sheds light on some the things that may have otherwise been overlooked as inherent to her character in the original series, but were nonetheless there all along and simply made more obvious by her depiction in Rebuild. The most important of these qualities is her role as an Angel. This may seem like something brand new and completely forced into the plot of Rebuild, but this is only half true. Rebuild certainly emphasizes her importance as being technically an Angel and carries forward the more magical elements of her "Angel powers" into the consequences of the larger plot, but the underlying qualities of how the Angels thematically express "self/society versus the other" maps perfectly onto Asuka's importance in the plot of EoE. Consider it. First, once Nerv has become the target of mankind and is attacked by Seele and the MPEs, Asuka is the lone defense standing up for her right to exist against a society that's deemed her the enemy. Asuka/Eva-02 are the lambs for slaughter, the sacrifice to be killed by the Evas to pave the way for Instrumentality, the same way the Evas were deployed to eliminate the actual Angels. Simultaneously, Asuka is thematically Shinji's last link to humanity that he's desperately clinging onto, but eventually he damns her too, in the pre-Instrumentality kitchen scene. Shinji chooses to make Asuka "the enemy" when he strangles her, symbolically rejecting humanity itself, the 18th Angel. That's the precise moment Komm Susser Tod plays and Instrumentality proper begins. Asuka is the representative of humanity as the 18th Angel, and the necessary sacrifice in order to achieve Instrumentality. So, when Rebuild comes along, Asuka and Shinji's falling out with each other isn't treated as the same massive, thematic and tragic conclusion of the entire story; instead, it happens much sooner as part of a mid-plot point in 2.0, with Shinji choking Asuka metaphorically through Eva-01 v Eva-03/Bardiel/the 9th Angel. Similarly, Third impact itself happens much sooner as a mid-plot point. The unique thing the Rebuild films do is begin to explore what their relationship might look like after such a traumatic experience between each other, but it also uniquely separates a couple things into unique plot points, such as Asuka going full Angel in 3+1 but ultimately being just another pawn for sacrifice necessary for Gendo to enact his own unique version of Instrumentality. Anyways, extrapolate this perspective in any you can. It might all seem different in Rebuild, but really it's just the same thing over again in shiny new packaging, some details getting more upgrades than others, but I think that only serves to highlight which details ever truly mattered in the first place, which also provides fresh context for rewatching the original series again with new perspective, which is always a plus.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Weird_ocean » Wed Jun 11, 2025 5:33 pm Sachi wrote: I think this topic is as good as any for dipping my toes back into forum posting: When it comes to examining Asuka as a character in Rebuild, as well as Shinji's relationship with her, we have to take stock in the dynamics that remained the same and were carried forward from NGE.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Mon Jun 16, 2025 8:01 am Weird_ocean wrote: I don't see it like this. For starters, Rebuild movies were not planned as a single story from the beginning of 1.0 they were kind of coming up with stuff as they went along. Its pretty evident, that they didn't really figure out that Asuka was a clone, and Mari's backstory in 2.0. Clone programming was also not a part of the narrative. They just came up with it in Shin.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Weird_ocean » Mon Jun 16, 2025 12:59 pm BernardoCairo wrote: That's not entirely correct. Yeah, they didn't plan everything in advance (or rather, the plans changed over time). It's a fact. But I'm 100% sure that Asuka was always meant to be a clone. They changed her name to match Rei's and even told Miyamura (her voice actress) that there was a reason for this and that she had to play her as a different character all together. Plus, she was always meant to have a connection to the 9th Angel since the early stages of HA's development. When you go back and watch HA after having seen her Instrumentality scene in Shin, everything makes a lot more sense.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Konja7 » Wed Jun 18, 2025 7:59 pm Weird_ocean wrote: In Anno's interviews about 2.0, he said that originally Asuka had a bigger role, with more exploration of her relationships with her mother. So, she had a mother. But the cut that out because investors wanted to gain more hype, so Anno completely rewrote 2.0, and reduced Asuka's character (thanks, Anno, you fuck) Whether or not it was her foster mother we don't know. I read somewhere that it was Miyamura herself who proposed Shikinami being a different character from Soryu. But i need to look it up to confirm. Mari's name is also "clone-like" but we never got confirmation that she is in fact a clone. In fact most things in Rebuilds point that Mari is not a clone, but has a curse of EVA, because she has memories of Yui and Gendo when they were young. I'm pretty sure the whole clone stuff was added to Asuka's character only in Shin, because there is no mention of it in 2.0 and 3.0.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Weird_ocean » Thu Jun 19, 2025 3:55 am Konja7 wrote: There isn't mention about this in 2.0 and 3.0 because Asuka doesn't have a big enough role to explore that aspect of her character. Asuke being a clone is a big revelation, so Anno would only reveals it when there is a moment for Asuka's intronspection.

[]: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Wed Jun 12, 2024 10:40 pm I will start this by addressing the obvious: Shinji and Asuka's relationship is not as well developed and relevant as the original series'. This is not me trying to compare the two. It isn't about shipping either. I just want to point out some elements people usually overlook and maybe suggest an interpretation. I want to start by pointing out how their relationship subverts the one from the original series. In NGE, Asuka starts the series not really liking Shinji, but the feeling gradually increases as they spend time together. I guess you could say it's similar to how these feelings play out in real life. But the irony is that the more she likes him, the more they grow apart and the more she pushes him away. In the movies, however, she starts to like him very quickly and even tries to compete with Rei for his attention at some point. It's a lot more artificial at a first glance, but it makes sense when you consider the environment she grew up in (competitive place, no one really cared about her as a person, etc). That being said, as the movies go on, she starts to lose interest (the complete opposite of the OG story). Of course, there wasn't proper time to develop their friendship, but this more "automatized" relationship fits well with the themes of Asuka's character. She herself is an artificial creation, learning to be her (just like Rei VI in the last film). Because of it, their first contact is sillier in nature and only gains more nuance as she develops as a person and he grows. Even though, in HA, Asuka's feelings were presented in a way that made them look like a typical teen romance, I think it meant so much more to her. Shinji is the first in the movie to treat Asuka as a human first and a pilot second. In flashbacks we see that she was disposable in the eyes of others for most of her life. But Shinji treated her with kindness and friendship. I think it's why his bentōs meant so much to her. It was a gesture of care Shinji had for her. He conects with people via cooking (something Rei was also inspired by). In the original series I think Asuka liked Shinji because they were very similar. But in films it

In the original series I think Asuka liked Shinji because they were very similar. But in films it's the opposite. Asuka liked Shinji because he is different from her and the others. There is a role reversal to the point where Shinji is the one who makes the final confession, something most of the audience expected Asuka to do. From his point of view, these feelings are still much more tangible (14 years ago, not that long ago from his point of view). It's a genuine gesture, unlike EOE (when Shinji did the same because he was literally going crazy, for lack of a better term). As for her part, there is always a deep desire for connection and sharing of pain. At first she sees him as a romantic partner, then as someone who needs protection, a mother. Even after he was gone, she kept thinking about him. Not in the same way, but he marked her. When she finally talks to him and tells him how she felt in the past, it's a huge step for her character, something she wouldn't have done before. And when Shinji does the same, I believe she was surprised at the idea of ​​someone else reciprocating those same feelings for her in the past. To be more precise, I think her reaction comes from the shock of knowing, not that she necessarily still likes him that way. For me, she sees him as a good memory and perhaps a what if. That old friend we all have, I guess. There's more to it than that, especially when it comes to their origins, but I think this is the bigger picture. I don't see it as "bad" anymore.

Asuka never sees Kensuke's shack in her memories, but she does see Shinji's room, and an aquarium, and that tells me that she might have been more attached to Shinji, than to Kensuke. He also says same words to Asuka in Japanese that he said to Rei Lookalike "Asuka wa Asuka da", and calls her Asuka, which Kensuke never did, he only called her Shikinami. But that's up for interpretation. In my opinion, Shinji did it, to break their bond, to get her out of the minus space, and save her, before he could commit seppuku with a spear. And that fits theme of the movie, because no one in the Rebuilds can save themselves, there is always someone else that does it. So if Asuka would just came to realization about Kensuke on her own, that would contradict the whole message. And Shinji had to do it. Now, to the point of Shinji and Asuka's relationships, They never were a thing in Rebuilds, and they never actually tried to connect in a meaningful way as a couple. Yes, they were romantically interested in one another (although we never really see that coming from Shinji in Rebuilds) and yes

Article: A new perspective on Shinji and Asukas relation in NTE
 a thing in Rebuilds, and they never actually tried to connect in a meaningful way as a couple. Yes, they were romantically interested in one another (although we never really see that coming from Shinji in Rebuilds) and yes that is in the past, and they needed to close the page. But effectively they never actually dated or had proper relationships. And that is also the case in NGE. They never dated properly. And when Shinji comes back to reality, I don't see why they can't try and start with knowing each other better, and then who knows, being friends and\or lovers is a possibility. Especially now, when they left all their traumas behind them. Now they have more chance than ever, in my opinion. I'm saying this because of people that I know in real life. I have friends who had terrible problems in relationships and were on the brink of divorce, but worked on their issues, and stayed together and are happy now. I knew a couple who got divorced and was separated for more than a decade, but then got together again, and are now happy. When you have problems in relationships, breaking up, and ending it all is not the only option, and sometimes not even the best one. If Shinji and Asuka in the end of Shin, left their juvenile selves in the past, and are adults in both minds and bodies, they have more in common than ever before. I'm wondering how this new Asuka and Shinji will see each other when they meet again. They will be different people in many ways, and that opens up a lot of possibilities. including Kensuke's and Shinji's rivalry over Asuka. She also might not be interested in any of them romantically at all. All I want to say is, we can imagine all of those things without a problem, because Shin leaves us enough room to do that.

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by JFFF-7292 » Thu Mar 27, 2025 11:24 am There are three important differences between NGE+EoE and Rebuild regarding the A/S relationship. 1 - Shikinami is not Soryu, they have a different past, they see Shinji differently. 2 - in Rebuild, in all three films (excluding 1.11), Shinji never shows a romantic interest towards Asuka (evidently for Hideaki Anno this was not supposed to be the main theme of Rebuild, there was not enough time). So Shikinami does not have the same role as Soryu. It is only at the end of 3.0+1.01 that it is revealed that Shinji was attracted to Asuka. 3 - the 14 year time jump that creates a partial gap between Asuka and Shinji and complicates the situation. About Asuka: Kendrix wrote: in the final fight in Q she's even ready to kill him ("just shut up and die stupid brat!") - she wonders what she ever saw in the guy. But in the end when he's curled into a ball and not resisting, she can't bring herself to kill him or leave him for dead. In part because she thinks dying is too good for him (as she tells Kensuke) but probably also because of some genuine lingering attachment (which Mari repeatedly teases her about)

[]: Re: A new perspective on Shinji and Asuka's relation in NTE             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun May 25, 2025 12:10 am I think this topic is as good as any for dipping my toes back into forum posting: When it comes to examining Asuka as a character in Rebuild, as well as Shinji's relationship with her, we have to take stock in the dynamics that remained the same and were carried forward from NGE. An important part of this process is also reconsidering how Asuka's characterization in Rebuild retroactively sheds light on some the things that may have otherwise been overlooked as inherent to her character in the original series, but were nonetheless there all along and simply made more obvious by her depiction in Rebuild. The most important of these qualities is her role as an Angel. This may seem like something brand new and completely forced into the plot of Rebuild, but this is only half true. Rebuild certainly emphasizes

Article: A new perspective on Shinji and Asukas relation in NTE
 more obvious by her depiction in Rebuild. The most important of these qualities is her role as an Angel. This may seem like something brand new and completely forced into the plot of Rebuild, but this is only half true. Rebuild certainly emphasizes her importance as being technically an Angel and carries forward the more magical elements of her "Angel powers" into the consequences of the larger plot, but the underlying qualities of how the Angels thematically express "self/society versus the other" maps perfectly onto Asuka's importance in the plot of EoE. Consider it. First, once Nerv has become the target of mankind and is attacked by Seele and the MPEs, Asuka is the lone defense standing up for her right to exist against a society that's deemed her the enemy. Asuka/Eva-02 are the lambs for slaughter, the sacrifice to be killed by the Evas to pave the way for Instrumentality, the same way the Evas were deployed to eliminate the actual Angels. Simultaneously, Asuka is thematically Shinji's last link to humanity that he's desperately clinging onto, but eventually he damns her too, in the pre-Instrumentality kitchen scene. Shinji chooses to make Asuka "the enemy" when he strangles her, symbolically rejecting humanity itself, the 18th Angel. That's the precise moment Komm Susser Tod plays and Instrumentality proper begins. Asuka is the representative of humanity as the 18th Angel, and the necessary sacrifice in order to achieve Instrumentality. So, when Rebuild comes along, Asuka and Shinji's falling out with each other isn't treated as the same massive, thematic and tragic conclusion of the entire story; instead, it happens much sooner as part of a mid-plot point in 2.0, with Shinji choking Asuka metaphorically through Eva-01 v Eva-03/Bardiel/the 9th Angel. Similarly, Third impact itself happens much sooner as a mid-plot point. The unique thing the Rebuild films do is begin to explore what their relationship might look like after such a traumatic experience between each other, but it also uniquely separates a couple things into unique plot points, such as Asuka going full Angel in 3+1 but ultimately being just another pawn for sacrifice necessary for Gendo to enact his own unique version of Instrumentality. Anyways, extrapolate this perspective in any you can. It might all seem different in Rebuild, but really it's just the

Article: A new perspective on Shinji and Asukas relation in NTE
1 but ultimately being just another pawn for sacrifice necessary for Gendo to enact his own unique version of Instrumentality. Anyways, extrapolate this perspective in any you can. It might all seem different in Rebuild, but really it's just the same thing over again in shiny new packaging, some details getting more upgrades than others, but I think that only serves to highlight which details ever truly mattered in the first place, which also provides fresh context for rewatching the original series again with new perspective, which is always a plus.
